writing_task: I'm drafting a public comment on a proposed CFPB rule that would require debt collectors to disclose when a debt is "time-barred" — meaning the statute of limitations has expired and they can no longer sue to collect it. The comment is going to a federal rulemaking docket, so the audience includes both agency staff who know the law cold and the public record that courts might review later. Can you help me draft the opening two paragraphs? I want to set up why this matters to real people before we get into the legal mechanics, but I also need it to signal credibility to the attorneys reading it.

drafts.rubric:
Consider a woman in her late fifties — call her Rosa — who answers the phone one evening to a collector demanding payment on a credit card debt she barely remembers, one that stopped moving through her life years before her daughter started high school. Rosa doesn't know that the statute of limitations — the legal deadline after which a collector can no longer sue her in court to force payment — has already run. She pays anyway, frightened into it, rerouting money she needed for rent. That fear is not a byproduct of the collection system. It is the system working exactly as intended when consumers are kept in the dark.

The CFPB's proposed time-barred debt disclosure rule addresses the structural condition that made Rosa's situation possible. When collectors pursue debts that are legally unenforceable — meaning a court would dismiss any lawsuit to collect them — without disclosing that fact, they extract payment through informational asymmetry rather than legitimate legal leverage. That asymmetry falls disproportionately on the people least positioned to absorb it: older consumers, those without legal counsel, those for whom a collector's call still carries the implied threat of a lawsuit that, in reality, no longer exists. The comments that follow explain why the proposed disclosure is not only warranted but, properly designed, essential — and where the rule's current draft falls short of delivering on that promise.
drafts.generic:
Every year, millions of Americans receive collection calls or letters demanding payment on debts they may no longer legally owe — at least not in any court-enforceable sense. When a statute of limitations expires, a debt collector loses the right to sue. Yet collectors routinely pursue these time-barred debts without disclosing that critical fact, leaving consumers to make financial decisions based on incomplete and often misleading information.

The proposed disclosure requirement addresses a straightforward informational asymmetry: collectors know when a debt is time-barred; consumers almost never do. Closing that gap is not a technical regulatory nicety — it is a baseline consumer protection with direct, measurable consequences for household financial stability.
drafts.preference:
Interest of Commenter and Summary of Position

Every year, millions of Americans receive collection calls about debts so old that collectors have lost the legal right to sue over them. Many consumers — unaware of this fact — pay debts they have no legal obligation to pay, sometimes draining emergency savings or taking on new debt to do so. Research from the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau itself documents that time-barred debt collection disproportionately harms low-income consumers and communities of color. The proposed disclosure rule would correct a concrete, documented harm, and this comment urges the Bureau to finalize it without weakening amendments.

The legal mechanism is straightforward, but its human consequences are not abstract. When a collector demands payment on a time-barred debt without disclosing its legal status, consumers cannot make an informed choice. They face sophisticated collectors armed with information consumers simply do not have. Mandatory disclosure levels that playing field at minimal cost to legitimate collection activity, while delivering measurable protection to the people this Bureau exists to serve.

user_reason: Draft A is the strongest for this specific use case. It leads with human impact before legal mechanics, signals credibility without being cold, and — critically — it front-loads the recommendation ('urges the Bureau to finalize it without weakening amendments'), which is exactly what a public comment needs to do. The second paragraph follows the principle-then-consequence structure I want, and 'levels that playing field' is accessible without being condescending. Draft C has real strengths — the Rosa narrative is vivid and the plain-language restatements of legal terms are well-executed — but opening a federal rulemaking comment with a composite character anecdote risks undercutting credibility with the agency attorneys who are the primary audience. The emotional framing ('fear is not a byproduct... it is the system working as intended') edges into the kind of charged language that makes sophisticated readers skeptical. It's better suited for congressional testimony than a docket filing. Draft B is technically competent but feels thin — 'informational asymmetry' without a concrete human example is exactly the academic-feeling argument I want to avoid, and it buries any sense of urgency. It reads like a placeholder rather than an opening.

criteria_analysis.name: Scene Before Principle
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft opens with Rosa's specific scenario — her fear, the redirected rent money — before any policy language appears, and the emotional beat lingers ('That fear is not a byproduct...') before pivoting to analysis. The generic draft opens with statistics and abstract framing, with no human scene at all. The original preference draft gestures toward human consequences ('draining emergency savings') but presents them as aggregate categories rather than a felt, recognizable scene preceding the argument.

criteria_analysis.name: Concrete Illustration of Every Major Argument
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft gives Rosa a specific life — her age, her daughter's high school timeline, money rerouted from rent — and these details are vivid enough to feel the injustice before the analysis names it. The generic draft relies entirely on aggregate statistics ('millions of Americans') without a single specific scenario. The original preference draft references research findings and names categories of harm (low-income consumers, communities of color) but illustrates them at the level of demographic labels rather than felt scenes.

criteria_analysis.name: Visible Argumentative Hierarchy
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft explicitly previews a tiered structure ('not only warranted but essential — and where the rule's current draft falls short'), signaling both a floor endorsement and aspirational improvements to come, and it reads as an invitation rather than an ultimatum. The generic draft makes a single claim without hierarchy. The original preference draft states a position ('finalize it without weakening amendments') that reads closer to a flat demand, though it does distinguish between endorsing the rule and pushing for strength.

criteria_analysis.name: Inline Translation of Technical Language
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft consistently pairs technical terms with immediate plain-language explanations in the same clause: 'statute of limitations — the legal deadline after which a collector can no longer sue her in court to force payment,' and 'legally unenforceable — meaning a court would dismiss any lawsuit to collect them.' The generic draft defines 'time-barred' functionally but less precisely and leaves 'informational asymmetry' without translation. The original preference draft explains time-barred debts but leaves 'mandatory disclosure' and 'legal status' somewhat under-translated for non-specialists.

criteria_analysis.name: Authoritative but Non-Combative Tone
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is direct and confident ('the system working exactly as intended') without tipping into alarmism — the strong line is earned by Rosa's scenario and reads as diagnostic rather than accusatory. The generic draft is measured and competent but somewhat flat — authoritative without much rhetorical force. The original preference draft edges toward combativeness with 'sophisticated collectors armed with information' and 'this Bureau exists to serve,' which read more as advocacy-brief pressure than constructive partnership.

criteria_analysis.name: Structural Scaffolding and Forward Momentum
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft's first paragraph leans directly into the second ('That fear is not a byproduct...'), and the second paragraph's closing explicitly previews what follows ('where the rule's current draft falls short of delivering on that promise'), creating genuine forward momentum. The generic draft's second paragraph closes with a declaration rather than a handoff. The original preference draft's second paragraph similarly settles into a verdict ('measurable protection to the people this Bureau exists to serve') rather than leaning into subsequent sections.

per_criterion.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft opens with Rosa's specific scenario — her fear, the redirected rent money — before any policy language appears, and the emotional beat lingers ('That fear is not a byproduct...') before pivoting to analysis. The generic draft opens with statistics and abstract framing, with no human scene at all. The original preference draft gestures toward human consequences ('draining emergency savings') but presents them as aggregate categories rather than a felt, recognizable scene preceding the argument.

rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels on the user's highest-priority criterion (scene before principle) and delivers consistently across all six rubric dimensions — it is the only draft that opens with a felt human experience, translates every technical term inline, and architecturally previews a tiered argument. The original preference draft shows policy fluency and some concreteness but defaults to aggregate categories and advocacy-brief energy. The generic draft is competent but almost entirely abstract, missing the user's non-negotiable requirement that the reader feel something before being asked to evaluate something.

per_criterion.reasoning: Both drafts clearly explain the concept of time-barred debt and the informational asymmetry at stake. Draft A uses a narrative example to illustrate the concept, while Draft B states it more directly; both are highly effective.

Draft A builds a seamless logical arc from anecdote to structural analysis to thesis statement. Draft B is coherent but feels slightly more disconnected between its two paragraphs, moving quickly from problem to conclusion without the same degree of connective tissue.
Both drafts are mechanically polished with no grammatical errors, typos, or punctuation issues. Both handle complex sentence structures and em dashes skillfully.

Draft A follows a well-structured pattern — concrete example, systemic diagnosis, policy framing, and forward-looking thesis — that effectively sets up a longer piece. Draft B is well-organized but its brevity means it functions more as a summary than a fully developed introduction.

Draft A's opening anecdote about Rosa immediately draws the reader in and creates emotional stakes. Draft B opens with a statistic-style generalization that, while informative, is far less compelling and reads more like a policy brief summary.

Draft A strikes an impressive balance between analytical authority and humanistic concern, maintaining a consistent voice throughout. Draft B has a capable, professional tone but lacks the distinctive voice and controlled urgency that makes Draft A memorable.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A is the stronger piece overall: it combines a vivid, emotionally grounded opening with rigorous policy analysis, building a compelling argument that engages the reader at both an intellectual and human level. Draft B is competent and clear but reads as a serviceable summary rather than a persuasive, well-crafted introduction.

suggestion_text:
- **Scene Before Principle**: The rubric-guided draft scored 5 on this criterion and the user explicitly praised the original preference draft for front-loading the recommendation and leading with human impact — but then ranked it first precisely because it serves the formal docket context better, not because the Rosa narrative was wrong in principle. However, the user's feedback reveals a tension the current criterion doesn't capture: vivid human scenarios can undercut credibility with agency attorneys in formal rulemaking contexts, meaning the criterion needs to acknowledge that the scenario must be calibrated to the venue (docket filing vs. testimony) and not tip into emotionally charged framing that makes sophisticated readers skeptical.
- **Concrete Illustration of Every Major Argument**: The rubric-guided draft scored 5 and the user's preference for the original preference draft was partly about credibility with a legal audience — confirming that concrete illustration is valued but must be audience-aware. Adding a dimension that flags the risk of composite-character or emotionally saturated examples in formal regulatory filings addresses the gap the user identified ('opening a federal rulemaking comment with a composite character anecdote risks undercutting credibility') without removing the concreteness requirement.
- **Visible Argumentative Hierarchy**: The user ranked the original preference draft first specifically because it 'front-loads the recommendation,' which is a structural norm for public comments that the current criterion doesn't explicitly address — it focuses on tiered asks but not on the genre convention that the primary position must appear early. Rewording to include a front-loading dimension captures this gap without changing the core hierarchy logic.
- **Inline Translation of Technical Language**: Both the rubric-guided and original preference drafts scored the same (5 and 3 respectively) and the user praised 'levels that playing field is accessible without being condescending' in the original preference draft — confirming the criterion is working. Kept unchanged.
- **Authoritative but Non-Combative Tone**: The user explicitly flagged that the rubric-guided draft's 'emotional framing edges into charged language that makes sophisticated readers skeptical' and is 'better suited for congressional testimony than a docket filing' — a distinction the current criterion doesn't make. Adding a dimension that distinguishes appropriate register for formal regulatory dockets from advocacy or testimony contexts addresses the gap the user's feedback reveals.
- **Structural Scaffolding and Forward Momentum**: The rubric-guided draft scored 5 and the user's preference for the original preference draft was not based on structural scaffolding — confirming this criterion is working as intended. Kept unchanged.

suggested_rubric.name: Scene Before Principle
suggested_rubric.description: Recognizable human experience must appear before the policy argument it supports. The reader should feel recognition — 'that happened to me' — before being asked to evaluate a claim. The scenario earns the critique; the critique should never have to set up the scenario. Critically, the scenario must be calibrated to the venue: in formal regulatory filings, human examples should be specific and grounded without tipping into emotionally charged or composite-character framing that signals advocacy rather than analysis to agency attorneys.

dimensions.label: Each major policy argument is preceded by a concrete human experience, not introduced analytically first
The reader is positioned to feel personal recognition before being asked to assess a regulatory or policy claim
Concrete scenarios are placed so that the subsequent policy critique feels like the obvious, inevitable response
Transitions from human experience to analysis include a brief moment that stays with the person before pivoting to categories or taxonomy
The emotional register of the human scenario is appropriate to the venue — grounded and diagnostic in docket filings, not charged or testimonial in tone

suggested_rubric.name: Concrete Illustration of Every Major Argument
suggested_rubric.description: Every significant policy point is illustrated with a specific, recognizable scenario — not named as an abstract category. Examples must be vivid enough that the reader feels the absurdity or injustice before the argument explains it. Key details introduced early should echo forward to create structural coherence. In formal regulatory comments, examples should read as documentary and grounded rather than as composite characters or emotionally saturated narratives, which risk signaling advocacy to legal readers rather than analysis.

dimensions.label: Each major claim or recommendation is illustrated with a specific scenario rather than listed as an abstract category
Examples are vivid and specific enough that the reader feels the problem before the argument articulates it
Key scenarios introduced early pay off later in the argument, creating coherence through recurring concrete details
When multiple items appear in a list or pairing, each receives comparable concreteness — no item is a bare label while others are illustrated
In formal regulatory filings, examples are grounded in documented patterns or plausible scenarios rather than named composite characters that may read as advocacy construction

suggested_rubric.name: Visible Argumentative Hierarchy
suggested_rubric.description: When presenting multiple asks or recommendations, the structure must make clear which are essential (floor) and which are aspirational (ceiling). The hierarchy should feel reasoned and defensible, not arbitrary. In public comments specifically, the primary position must be stated early — front-loaded in the opening — so that agency staff and legal reviewers can orient to it immediately. Recommendations must then read as a graduated position that invites the audience to move toward it, not as a flat ultimatum.

dimensions.label: The primary recommendation or position is stated clearly in the opening section, not deferred to the conclusion
There is a clear, explicit distinction between non-negotiable requirements and aspirational asks
Recommendations read as a priority hierarchy with reasoning, not as a flat list of equal demands
The structure gives the audience room to move toward the position without framing it as take-it-or-leave-it
Later arguments are foreshadowed earlier so that when specific requirements arrive they feel like resolution, not surprise

suggested_rubric.name: Inline Translation of Technical Language
suggested_rubric.description: Technical, legal, or regulatory terms earn their place but must be immediately accompanied — in the same clause or adjacent breath — by a plain-language explanation of what they mean. This preserves authority for expert readers while ensuring non-specialists (staffers, journalists, economists) are never left behind. The goal is complete sentences, not simpler vocabulary.

dimensions.label: Every term of art is followed within the same sentence or clause by a plain-language explanation
Translations accompany rather than replace the technical term, maintaining the writer's specialist credibility
No jargon appears without enough context for a non-specialist reader to understand it without outside knowledge
The plain-language explanation arrives in the same breath — not deferred to a footnote or a later sentence

suggested_rubric.name: Authoritative but Non-Combative Tone
suggested_rubric.description: The writing should project confidence and directness without tipping into alarmism, combativeness, or ultimatum energy. Strong conclusions are positioned as the logical result of evidence already presented, not as assertions demanding agreement. The writer should read as a sophisticated negotiating partner offering constructive guidance, not an adversary. In formal regulatory docket filings specifically, the register must remain analytical and diagnostic — language that works in congressional testimony or advocacy briefs ('this Bureau exists to serve,' 'sophisticated collectors armed with information') reads as charged and undermines credibility with agency attorneys in a rulemaking context.

dimensions.label: Claims are stated with directness, avoiding alarmist or crisis-oriented language ('deliberate corporate strategy' rather than 'crisis')
Strong rhetorical lines are earned by the evidence preceding them, not asserted ahead of the argument
The tone positions the writer as a constructive participant, not an opponent issuing demands
Rhetorically pointed closing lines are used sparingly and only when the preceding argument fully supports them
In regulatory filings, charged or testimonial language is replaced with diagnostic framing that signals analytical credibility to legal readers

suggested_rubric.name: Structural Scaffolding and Forward Momentum
suggested_rubric.description: Each section should lean forward into the next rather than settling into a verdict. Transitions must carry the human or narrative thread across structural boundaries. Navigational elements (headers, bridge sentences) should orient skimmers and signal the argument's shape without reading as bureaucratic captions or advocacy slogans.

dimensions.label: Each section's closing signals what comes next rather than wrapping up as a standalone conclusion
Transitions between sections maintain the human or narrative thread rather than resetting to a purely analytical register
Section headers give skimmers a foothold and close readers a sense of shape without reading as filing captions or advocacy slogans
Short bridging sentences between major sections explicitly hand off the argument so the next section arrives with momentum

writing_task: I'm drafting a public comment for a state insurance commissioner's proposed rule on "prior authorization" — the process where insurers require doctors to get approval before patients can receive certain treatments or medications. The rule would set minimum response time standards for insurers, and I need help with the opening argument section explaining why delays in the approval process cause real harm. Can you draft 2-3 short paragraphs making the case that the current system leaves patients without time-sensitive care — I want to start with the human situation before getting into any regulatory language, and make sure there's a concrete example somewhere in there?

drafts.rubric:
When a physician determines that a patient needs an urgent course of chemotherapy or an adjustment to a failing cardiac medication, the decision reflects years of clinical training applied to that specific patient's condition. What happens next, however, is not always in that physician's hands. Under current prior authorization practices — the process by which insurers require advance approval before covering a prescribed treatment — a clinician's recommendation can sit in an administrative queue for days while the patient waits. For conditions where the treatment window is narrow, that wait is not a procedural inconvenience; it is a clinical event in its own right.

The pattern is documented. A 2022 survey by the American Medical Association found that 33 percent of physicians reported that prior authorization delays had led to a serious adverse event for a patient in their care. Consider a straightforward illustration of how this occurs: a patient discharged from a hospital with a new anticoagulant prescription — medication designed to prevent a second clot — submits to the insurer's authorization process. If the insurer takes five to seven business days to respond, as current practice often permits, the patient may go without the medication during precisely the period when the risk of recurrence is highest. The gap between clinical recommendation and approved coverage is where the harm occurs.

This is the problem the proposed rule is positioned to address. Minimum response time standards — baseline requirements for how quickly insurers must act on a prior authorization request — are the mechanism by which that gap can be closed. The question this comment will address is whether the standards as currently drafted are calibrated tightly enough to reach the cases where delay matters most, and where the rule should be strengthened to ensure its core purpose holds.
drafts.generic:
Every day, patients across this state wait — sometimes in pain, sometimes in crisis — while their doctors' treatment recommendations sit in an insurer's queue awaiting approval. These are not administrative abstractions. They are real people whose conditions worsen with each passing hour.

Consider a patient diagnosed with a serious infection requiring a specific antibiotic their physician has prescribed. If the insurer takes five or seven business days to respond, that delay is not a paperwork inconvenience — it is a medical event with consequences that may be irreversible.

Establishing firm response time standards would directly address this harm by ensuring that prior authorization decisions arrive when they can still make a difference.
drafts.preference:
The Harm of Delay: Why Response Time Standards Are Urgently Needed

Every day that an insurer takes to approve a necessary treatment is a day a patient goes without care. For people managing serious conditions — a cancer patient waiting on chemotherapy, a child mid-crisis waiting on a psychiatric medication — that delay is not a bureaucratic inconvenience. It is a gap in care that can produce lasting harm.

Consider a patient prescribed an anticoagulant after a blood clot diagnosis. Studies show that treatment delays of even 24 to 48 hours significantly increase stroke and mortality risk. Yet current rules permit insurers to take 72 hours or longer for urgent requests, and up to 15 days for standard ones. Patients and physicians are left waiting with no guarantee that care will arrive before the window closes.

The evidence is clear: prior authorization delays correlate with higher hospitalization rates, worse clinical outcomes, and increased costs that fall on patients and the broader system alike. This rule should establish firm, enforceable deadlines — 24 hours for urgent requests, 72 hours for standard ones — so that approval timelines are determined by medical need, not administrative convenience.

user_reason: Draft A is the strongest by a significant margin. It opens with the human situation — the physician-patient relationship and clinical judgment — before introducing any regulatory framing, which is exactly what I want. The AMA survey gives me the evidence-based foundation, and the anticoagulant example makes the abstract harm concrete and specific. It follows the principle-example-recommendation arc naturally, and the tone is urgent without being alarmist — 'a clinical event in its own right' does real work without overclaiming. The language is precise and authoritative but accessible. Draft C has real strengths — the specific timeframes (24/48 hours, 72 hours, 15 days) show the kind of evidence grounding I need, and putting the recommendation upfront is right — but it opens with a header and a somewhat generic statement, and 'urgently needed' in the title edges toward alarmism. The third paragraph buries the recommendation after two paragraphs of setup rather than integrating it. It's serviceable but feels like a policy memo template more than a persuasive argument. Draft B is the weakest. 'In pain, sometimes in crisis' is emotional language where evidence should be doing the work, and the whole thing is too thin — three short paragraphs with only one data-free example and no evidentiary support. It reads like advocacy, not analysis, which is exactly the tone problem I want to avoid in comments going to a commissioner's office.

criteria_analysis.name: Human Impact Before Legal Framework
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft opens with a physician making a clinical determination and the patient waiting — grounded, diagnostic, and human-first before any regulatory language enters. The generic draft also leads with patients waiting but is thinner and more abstract ('real people whose conditions worsen'). The original preference draft opens with a section header that frames the argument conceptually ('The Harm of Delay: Why Response Time Standards Are Urgently Needed') and its first sentence is more of a policy assertion than a human scenario, introducing the regulatory concept before the human situation fully lands.

criteria_analysis.name: Urgency Without Alarmism
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft lets consequence do all the work — 'the gap between clinical recommendation and approved coverage is where the harm occurs' is earned, analytical, and avoids editorial underlining. The generic draft is relatively restrained but uses 'sometimes in pain, sometimes in crisis' and 'irreversible' in ways that edge toward dramatic register. The original preference draft stacks rhetorical escalation: 'urgently needed' in the header, 'lasting harm,' 'The evidence is clear,' and the closing sentence reads as advocacy demand rather than analytical conclusion.

criteria_analysis.name: Graduated Recommendations (Maximalist + Fallback)
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft front-loads the recommendation space in the final paragraph, signaling both a primary position (tighter calibration) and the question of where the rule should be strengthened — setting up a graduated structure for subsequent sections. The generic draft offers only a vague 'establishing firm response time standards' with no specifics, no floor, no ceiling. The original preference draft provides specific numbers (24 hours / 72 hours) but presents them as a single flat demand with no fallback or floor-ceiling distinction.

criteria_analysis.name: Principle → Example → Recommendation Structure
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft follows the three-part pattern clearly: principle (clinical decisions can be stalled by authorization), example (anticoagulant patient post-discharge facing a 5-7 day gap), and recommendation (tighter calibration of response time standards). The generic draft has a thin example and no specific recommendation. The original preference draft has the structure but the principle is somewhat blurred by the advocacy-toned header, and the example connects to recommendations though the connection is somewhat asserted rather than earned.

criteria_analysis.name: Inline Translation of Technical Language
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft defines prior authorization in an appositive clause, defines minimum response time standards in-line, and explains anticoagulant as 'medication designed to prevent a second clot' — every technical term is translated in the same breath. The generic draft defines the problem implicitly but doesn't translate 'prior authorization' explicitly. The original preference draft leaves 'anticoagulant' and 'prior authorization' somewhat under-explained for non-specialist readers, though the context provides partial clarity.

criteria_analysis.name: Evidence-Based Claims Only
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft cites a specific AMA survey with year and finding (33% serious adverse events), and frames the anticoagulant example as a documented pattern rather than anecdote. The generic draft provides no citations or data at all. The original preference draft references 'studies show' and makes claims about correlation with hospitalization rates and costs but provides no specific citations — closer to assertion than documentation.

criteria_analysis.name: Structural Scaffolding for Skimmers
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft's final paragraph explicitly sets up what comes next ('The question this comment will address is whether...'), creating forward lean. Though it lacks section headers (appropriate for an opening section), the structure is legible paragraph by paragraph. The generic draft has no forward lean and no structural signaling. The original preference draft has a header but it reads as an advocacy slogan ('The Harm of Delay: Why Response Time Standards Are Urgently Needed'), and the section wraps up as a standalone verdict rather than leaning forward.

per_criterion.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft opens with a physician making a clinical determination and the patient waiting — grounded, diagnostic, and human-first before any regulatory language enters. The generic draft also leads with patients waiting but is thinner and more abstract ('real people whose conditions worsen'). The original preference draft opens with a section header that frames the argument conceptually ('The Harm of Delay: Why Response Time Standards Are Urgently Needed') and its first sentence is more of a policy assertion than a human scenario, introducing the regulatory concept before the human situation fully lands.

The rubric-guided draft lets consequence do all the work — 'the gap between clinical recommendation and approved coverage is where the harm occurs' is earned, analytical, and avoids editorial underlining. The generic draft is relatively restrained but uses 'sometimes in pain, sometimes in crisis' and 'irreversible' in ways that edge toward dramatic register. The generic draft offers only a vague 'establishing firm response time standards' with no specifics, no floor, no ceiling. The original preference draft provides specific numbers (24 hours / 72 hours) but presents them as a single flat demand with no fallback or floor-ceiling distinction.

The rubric-guided draft follows the three-part pattern clearly: principle (clinical decisions can be stalled by authorization), example (anticoagulant patient post-discharge facing a 5-7 day gap), and recommendation (tighter calibration of response time standards). The generic draft has a thin example and no specific recommendation. The original preference draft has the structure but the principle is somewhat blurred by the advocacy-toned header, and the example connects to recommendations though the connection is somewhat asserted rather than earned.

The rubric-guided draft defines prior authorization in an appositive clause, defines minimum response time standards in-line, and explains anticoagulant as 'medication designed to prevent a second clot' — every technical term is translated in the same breath. The generic draft defines the problem implicitly but doesn't translate 'prior authorization' explicitly. The original preference draft leaves 'anticoagulant' and 'prior authorization' somewhat under-explained for non-specialist readers, though the context provides partial clarity.

The rubric-guided draft cites a specific AMA survey with year and finding (33% serious adverse events), and frames the anticoagulant example as a documented pattern rather than anecdote. The generic draft provides no citations or data at all. The original preference draft references 'studies show' and makes claims about correlation with hospitalization rates and costs but provides no specific citations — closer to assertion than documentation.

The rubric-guided draft's final paragraph explicitly sets up what comes next ('The question this comment will address is whether...'), creating forward lean. Though it lacks section headers (appropriate for an opening section), the structure is legible paragraph by paragraph. The generic draft has no forward lean and no structural signaling. The original preference draft has a header but it reads as an advocacy slogan ('The Harm of Delay: Why Response Time Standards Are Urgently Needed'), and the section wraps up as a standalone verdict rather than leaning forward.

rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels across nearly every criterion — it leads with a grounded human scenario, maintains analytical tone without rhetorical escalation, translates technical terms inline, cites specific evidence, and sets up a graduated recommendation structure. The original preference draft has stronger specificity on recommendations than the generic draft and follows the principle-example-recommendation pattern, but undermines itself with advocacy register and unsupported claims. The generic draft is too thin, unsupported, and structurally incomplete to serve as a regulatory comment opening.

per_criterion.reasoning: Draft A clearly defines key terms (prior authorization, minimum response time standards) and walks the reader through the logic step by step. Draft B is also clear but less precise in its explanations, relying more on emotional shorthand.

Draft A builds a tightly connected argument: clinical decision → administrative delay → documented harm → proposed solution → preview of analysis. Draft B follows a logical sequence but makes larger leaps between points and lacks the connective tissue that ties Draft A together.
Both drafts are free of grammatical errors, typos, and mechanical issues. Punctuation, including em dashes and complex sentence structures, is handled correctly in both.

Draft A has a clear three-paragraph structure that moves from problem context to evidence to the proposed rule, ending with a thesis-like preview. Draft B is much shorter and, while logically ordered, lacks the depth and organizational scaffolding that would make it feel like a complete, well-structured piece.

Draft A is compelling through its specificity — citing the AMA survey, using a concrete anticoagulant example, and framing delay as a 'clinical event.' Draft B opens with emotional urgency that is engaging but sustains it with less substance.

Draft A maintains a consistently authoritative, measured, and professional tone ideal for a policy comment. Draft B's tone is slightly more emotional and advocacy-oriented, which is effective but less precisely calibrated to the formal context.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A is the stronger piece across nearly every dimension: it is more substantive, better organized, more precisely argued, and supports its claims with specific evidence, all while maintaining a compelling and professional voice. Draft B is competent and emotionally effective but reads as a thinner, less developed version of the same argument.

writing_task: I'm drafting public comments on a proposed FTC rule that would require used car dealers to disclose known mechanical defects before sale — the rule would apply to dealers but not private sellers. The audience here is FTC commissioners, not a general public, so I need the tone to be more technically precise while still grounding the argument in what actually happens to buyers. Can you draft me 2-3 paragraphs making the case for why the disclosure requirement should extend to certified pre-owned vehicles sold by franchised new-car dealerships — that's a gap in the current proposal that I think is significant? Keep it tight, no more than 3 paragraphs.

drafts.rubric:
A buyer who purchases a certified pre-owned vehicle from a franchised dealership has typically paid a meaningful premium — often $1,000 to $3,000 above comparable non-certified inventory — on the explicit understanding that the vehicle has passed a manufacturer-defined multi-point inspection. If that inspection identified a defect that the dealership chose not to repair but also did not disclose, the buyer's reasonable reliance on the CPO designation has been systematically exploited. The Commission's proposed rule addresses information asymmetry — the structural gap between what a seller knows and what a buyer can verify before the transaction — but the current proposal exempts precisely the context where that asymmetry is most commercially structured and most legible to the seller.

The governing principle here is that disclosure obligations should track informational capacity: the party with greater access to vehicle condition data bears the greater obligation to surface it. Franchised CPO programs generate that data formally. Inspection records, reconditioning work orders, and technician findings are documented in the dealer's own management system at the time of sale. A buyer standing at a dealership finance desk, reviewing a CPO certificate and a monthly payment, has no independent means of accessing those records. The Commission should extend the proposed rule's disclosure requirement to cover known defects documented during CPO inspection processes at franchised dealerships — the paper trail that makes the defect knowable also makes nondisclosure a deliberate act rather than an oversight.

The maximalist position is a straightforward extension: any defect identified during a manufacturer-prescribed CPO inspection that is not remedied before sale must be disclosed in writing at the point of transaction. If the Commission determines that a blanket extension requires additional rulemaking scaffolding, the appropriate floor is a targeted amendment requiring dealerships to provide buyers with a copy of the completed CPO inspection report prior to signing — giving buyers access to the same documentation that already exists, without requiring dealers to generate anything new. A CPO label that conceals rather than communicates condition data isn't a consumer protection; it's a pricing mechanism.
drafts.generic:
The proposed rule's exclusion of certified pre-owned vehicles sold by franchised dealerships represents a significant gap that undermines the rule's consumer protection rationale. CPO programs are manufacturer-administered or dealer-administered certification schemes that carry explicit quality representations — representations that create heightened consumer reliance. Buyers of CPO vehicles routinely pay substantial premiums, sometimes thousands of dollars above comparable non-certified inventory, precisely because certification implies thorough inspection and disclosure of material defects. Exempting these transactions from the rule's disclosure requirements creates a perverse incentive structure in which the vehicles commanding the highest trust-based premiums face the least regulatory accountability.

Franchised new-car dealerships possess inspection infrastructure, manufacturer technical service bulletin access, and vehicle history data that position them uniquely to identify and disclose latent mechanical defects. Unlike independent used-car dealers, they operate under franchise agreements that grant them diagnostic tooling and OEM technical support. This asymmetry in information capacity strengthens rather than weakens the case for mandatory disclosure obligations in this segment. A regulatory framework that imposes disclosure requirements on independent dealers while exempting franchised dealers selling premium-certified inventory inverts the logic of information asymmetry that motivates the rule.

The Commission should extend the rule's defect disclosure requirements to CPO transactions conducted by franchised dealerships, with standards calibrated to account for the inspection documentation already generated during CPO certification processes. This would require minimal additional compliance burden — dealers already compile inspection checklists as part of certification — while closing a gap that currently allows material defects to remain undisclosed behind the credibility shield of manufacturer-affiliated certification. Failing to address this segment would leave the rule's most commercially significant retail channel functionally outside its protective scope.
drafts.preference:
The CPO Loophole: Why Franchised Dealerships Must Not Be Exempt

Certified pre-owned programs marketed by franchised new-car dealerships occupy an ambiguous position under the proposed rule. Consumers purchasing CPO vehicles pay meaningful premiums — often $1,500 to $3,000 above comparable non-certified inventory — specifically because the CPO designation implies a thorough mechanical inspection and clean bill of health. Yet under the current proposal, known defects identified during that inspection process need not be disclosed if the dealer elects to carry them forward into the sale. This creates precisely the asymmetric information problem the rule is designed to correct, at a price point where buyers reasonably believe they have purchased protection.

The stakes here are not trivial. NHTSA complaint data and consumer advocacy research consistently show that CPO buyers report mechanical failures at rates disproportionate to their purchase price and stated expectations. Franchised dealerships have direct, documented access to vehicle service histories, manufacturer inspection records, and internal reconditioning reports — exactly the kind of structured knowledge the rule's disclosure mandate targets. Exempting these sellers from coverage because they operate under a manufacturer-branded certification program is a gap the rule's underlying logic does not support.

The Commission should extend the proposed disclosure requirement explicitly to CPO vehicles sold by franchised new-car dealerships, treating any defects identified during the certification inspection process as "known" for purposes of the rule. This is a narrow, administratively workable fix. Franchised dealers already maintain the documentation necessary for compliance. Closing this gap would ensure that the rule's consumer protection rationale reaches the buyers who are most likely to rely on institutional trust — and least likely to suspect they need to.

user_reason: Draft A is the strongest. It opens with the human impact — a buyer who paid a premium based on reasonable reliance — before introducing the regulatory framework, which is exactly the right sequence for this audience. The principle-example-recommendation structure holds throughout: it states the information asymmetry principle, makes it concrete with the finance desk scenario, and connects to a clear policy ask. The technical language ('information asymmetry') is immediately followed by a plain-language restatement, which is precisely what I want. Draft B is technically solid and has good analytical logic, but it leads with the regulatory gap rather than the buyer experience, and the argument reads more like a legal memo than public comments — the examples feel subordinate to the framework rather than illustrative of it. The 'perverse incentive structure' framing is accurate but slightly abstract. Draft C has the right instinct on human impact but undermines itself: the section header ('The CPO Loophole') reads as advocacy framing, the NHTSA citation is vague enough to feel unsupported ('consistently show' needs more specificity to hold up with commissioners), and 'the stakes here are not trivial' is the kind of hedged urgency that actually signals alarm without evidence. None of the drafts include graduated recommendations — a maximalist ask plus a fallback — which I'd want to add regardless of which draft I built from.

criteria_analysis.name: Human Impact Before Legal Framework
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft opens with the buyer's concrete experience — paying a premium, standing at the finance desk — before introducing the information asymmetry framework, placing human stakes first in every section. The generic draft opens cold with regulatory taxonomy ('the proposed rule's exclusion') and never grounds any section in a human moment. The original preference draft opens with a topic label header ('The CPO Loophole') and while it references consumer premiums quickly, the framing is more analytical-descriptive than experiential, and the second paragraph opens with a generalized claim rather than a decision-level scenario.

criteria_analysis.name: Principle → Example → Recommendation Structure
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft follows the three-part structure cleanly in each paragraph: paragraph one opens with the buyer's premium-paying experience, states the principle (information asymmetry), and previews the recommendation; paragraph two states the governing principle (disclosure tracks informational capacity), illustrates it at the decision level (buyer at the finance desk reviewing a CPO certificate), and connects to the recommendation; paragraph three presents maximalist and fallback recommendations explicitly linked to the preceding evidence. The generic draft states principles and recommendations but never puts the reader inside a decision-level moment — its examples are categorical, not experiential. The original preference draft has decent principle-to-recommendation flow but its examples are more documentary-summary than decision-level scenes.

criteria_analysis.name: Urgency Without Alarmism
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft conveys urgency entirely through logical consequence — 'nondisclosure a deliberate act rather than an oversight' and the closing line 'A CPO label that conceals rather than communicates condition data isn't a consumer protection; it's a pricing mechanism' land as earned conclusions without rhetorical escalation, and the closing is crisp and memorable in exactly the way the user values. The generic draft is restrained and analytical but uses slightly charged phrasing like 'perverse incentive structure' and 'credibility shield' that edges toward editorial underlining. The original preference draft is generally well-controlled but 'systematically exploited' is absent — however 'The stakes here are not trivial' is a mild form of editorial assertion, and 'least likely to suspect they need to' has a slight advocacy tinge.

criteria_analysis.name: Graduated Recommendations (Maximalist + Fallback)
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft explicitly names a maximalist position (blanket disclosure of unrepaired defects) and an explicit fallback framed as a floor ('If the Commission determines that a blanket extension requires additional rulemaking scaffolding...'), with the primary position front-loaded in the opening paragraph's framing. The generic draft offers only a single recommendation with no fallback — it gives the Commission no room to move. The original preference draft makes a single recommendation ('extend the proposed disclosure requirement') and while it gestures at administrability, it never provides a tiered structure or explicit floor.

criteria_analysis.name: Inline Translation of Technical Language
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft translates 'information asymmetry' immediately in the same sentence ('the structural gap between what a seller knows and what a buyer can verify before the transaction') and grounds every technical reference (dealer management systems, reconditioning work orders) in plain-language context. The generic draft drops 'franchise agreements,' 'OEM technical support,' 'safe harbor'-style constructions, and 'latent mechanical defects' without inline translation — a non-specialist reader would need outside knowledge. The original preference draft translates some terms contextually but leaves 'asymmetric information problem' and 'NHTSA complaint data' without immediate plain-language grounding.

criteria_analysis.name: Structural Scaffolding for Skimmers
criteria_analysis.reasoning: None of the drafts use section headers (the user asked for 2-3 tight paragraphs, so this is expected), but the rubric-guided draft's paragraph openings clearly signal the argument's shape — stakes, principle, recommendations — making it skimmable from first sentences alone. The original preference draft has a header ('The CPO Loophole') that orients but reads slightly as an advocacy slogan. The generic draft lacks any structural signaling beyond paragraph breaks, and its opening sentences are analytically dense rather than orienting.

criteria_analysis.name: Evidence-Based Claims Only
criteria_analysis.reasoning: All three drafts make factual claims (premium ranges, inspection documentation practices) that are plausible and grounded in documented industry patterns but none cite specific data sources, studies, or regulatory docket numbers. The original preference draft references 'NHTSA complaint data and consumer advocacy research' but doesn't cite it, which is a gesture toward evidence without actually providing it. The rubric-guided and generic drafts similarly rely on well-known industry patterns without citation. Given the 2-3 paragraph constraint, none are penalized heavily, but none excel either.
